title: Today's AI Brief: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
summary: Exploring the latest developments in AI, from the Vatican's unprecedented alliance with Anthropic to the chaos caused by AI agents in the tech world.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageCaption: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageSourceTitle: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation

body.text: I'm looking at the latest news in AI, and I have to say, it's been an interesting week. The Vatican, of all places, has been making headlines with its foray into the world of artificial intelligence. Pope Leo XIV recently presented his first encyclical on AI, and in a surprising move, he invited Christopher Olah, cofounder of Anthropic, to speak. This unlikely alliance between the Catholic Church and Silicon Valley has raised some eyebrows, and I'm curious to learn more about what brought these two worlds together.

So, what's behind this unexpected partnership? According to reports, the Vatican is looking to Anthropic to help shape its understanding of AI and its potential impact on society. It's a bold move, considering the Catholic Church's historical skepticism towards technological advancements. But as AI continues to evolve and become more integrated into our daily lives, it's clear that the Church wants to stay ahead of the curve. And who better to guide them than one of the leading minds in the field?

But the Vatican's not the only one grappling with the implications of AI. A recent article in Technology Review highlights the disconnect between ambition and execution when it comes to adopting agentic AI. It seems that while 85% of organizations want to become 'agentic' within the next three years, 76% admit that their current infrastructure can't support such a change. It's a bit like trying to put a square peg in a round hole – it just won't fit. And that's where the chaos comes in.

Speaking of chaos, have you heard about the 'Claude Code Anonymous' meetup in London? It's a support group for techies who've become, well, a bit too enamored with coding tools like Anthropic's Claude. One attendee, Peter Steinberger, joked that he's a 'Claudeholic.' It's a lighthearted take on a serious issue – the potential for AI to disrupt our lives in unforeseen ways. And it's not just individuals who are affected; the entire tech world has been turned on its head by the rise of AI agents.

They cite a lack of readiness across... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

As I wrap up my notes on the latest AI developments, I'm struck by the sheer scope of change that's underway. From the Vatican to Silicon Valley, it's clear that AI is no longer just a niche interest – it's a global phenomenon. And while there are certainly challenges to be addressed, I'm heartened by the sense of curiosity and collaboration that's driving innovation in this space. Who knows what the future holds? One thing's for sure – it'll be interesting to watch.
